questions.question: According to Gallup research, how much more engaged are employees who have a 'best friend at work'?

options.text: 2x more engaged
4x more engaged
7x more engaged
10x more engaged

questions.explanation: Gallup found employees with a 'best friend at work' are 7x more engaged, complete tasks 50% faster through collaboration ease, and show 50% reduction in turnover due to social connection to the organization. However, these benefits only materialize when appropriate boundaries prevent favoritism, gossip, and conflicts of interest. Workplace friendships are powerful for engagement and retention, but require intentional boundary management to avoid compromising professional judgment.

questions.question: What are the three boundary management strategies for workplace relationships?

options.text: Avoidance, Openness, Transparency
Openness, Indirect Refocus, Direct Refocus
Disclosure, Privacy, Neutrality
Sharing, Deflecting, Ignoring

questions.explanation: The three strategies are: OPENNESS (voluntarily sharing personal information to build connection), INDIRECT REFOCUS (deflecting personal questions without explicitly refusing—'Nothing too exciting! How was YOUR weekend?'), and DIRECT REFOCUS (explicitly declining to share—'I prefer to keep my relationship private'). Successful professionals use primarily openness with selective refocus (not rigid walls or oversharing), adjusting strategy based on trust, power dynamics, context, comfort level, and risk.

questions.question: According to the disclosure traffic light system, which topics are 'yellow light' (context-dependent) requiring careful assessment before sharing at work?

options.text: Weekend activities and hobbies
Mental health challenges, relationship difficulties, and financial stress
Intimate relationship details and illegal activities
Professional challenges and career goals

questions.explanation: Yellow light topics—mental health challenges, relationship difficulties, financial stress, political/religious views, health issues affecting work—depend on trust, workplace culture, power dynamics, and whether sharing helps or creates vulnerability. Share with trusted individuals privately rather than broadcasting to entire team. Green light topics (weekend activities, values, professional challenges) are generally safe. Red light topics (intimate details, illegal activities, gossip, discriminatory views) are high risk with minimal benefit and should be avoided except when disclosing to HR/supervisor for accommodation needs.

questions.question: What is the appropriate strategy when your close work friend gets promoted to become your direct supervisor?

options.text: Assume the friendship means you'll get special treatment and expect leniency
End the friendship entirely to avoid any appearance of favoritism

Have explicit conversation about separating roles—'Our friendship matters AND I want you to succeed as supervisor. Let's separate professional during work, friends outside. Hold me to same standards as everyone.'
Continue exactly as before and hope it works out without addressing the role change

questions.explanation: The evidence-based approach is explicitly naming the dual relationship, expressing care for both roles, and establishing clear boundaries. This creates shared understanding about separating professional standards during work from friendship outside work, inviting professional accountability (not expecting leniency), and protecting both the friendship and professional relationship. Expecting special treatment creates favoritism and team resentment. Ending friendship is overly rigid. Avoiding the conversation creates confusion and differing expectations that damage both relationships.

questions.question: If you supervise others and make decisions affecting a close friend's promotion, assignments, or evaluation, what is the most appropriate action to prevent actual and perceived favoritism?

options.text: Give your friend preferential treatment since you know they deserve it
Be extra harsh on your friend to prove you're not playing favorites
Document rationale for decisions with legitimate work reasons, maintain equal treatment standards, and recuse yourself from decisions where relationship compromises objectivity
Hide the friendship from everyone so they don't know about the conflict

questions.explanation: Preventing favoritism requires: decision transparency (explain criteria), documentation (record legitimate work rationale), equal treatment (give friend SAME feedback/accountability as others—not leniency or over-harshness), and recusal when appropriate (remove yourself from decisions where you can't be objective). The question 'Would I make this same decision for someone I'm NOT friends with?' tests for bias. Both preferential treatment and over-correction create unfairness. Hiding the relationship prevents transparency and accountability. Professional integrity requires honest assessment of when friendship compromises judgment.

title: Professional vs Personal Relationships — Navigating Dual Relationships - Quiz
